vix.note: The VIX slipped to 18.03, down about 3 percent from a prior close near 18.6 and far below its year high of 35, signaling that Wednesday's rotation out of AI and out of havens was an orderly de-risking rather than a fear event.

macro: Micron's after-close blowout capped an orderly de-risking day as both the Mideast risk premium and the gold-silver-bitcoin debasement trade unwound. Semis stabilized on Wednesday (SMH -0.5 percent, NVDA -0.5 percent) and after the bell Micron crushed fiscal-Q3 results, validating the AI-memory super-cycle and sparking an Asia chip rally into Thursday, with SK Hynix adding fuel via plans for a roughly $30 billion US listing. The bigger moves were elsewhere: with the Strait of Hormuz reopened and US-Iran tensions easing, oil sank 4.5 percent (USO $106) while gold fell 3.4 percent below $4,000 and silver dropped 6.4 percent below $60 as a hawkish Fed repricing, now leaning toward up to three rate hikes, lifted the dollar to a one-year high. The VIX actually fell to 18, confirming this was money rotating out of high-flying AI and crowded havens into beaten-down cyclicals rather than a panic.
action: Let the metals and oil washout run before bottom-fishing: hold quality ballast (BRKB, MSFT, DIA, SPY), watch semis (SMH, NVDA, AVGO) for a Micron-fueled base instead of chasing the Asia bounce, and treat the hawkish Fed repricing and stronger dollar as the key macro risk for gold, bitcoin and rate-sensitive growth.

long.h: Micron Blows Doors Off Q3 Earnings, Validating the AI-Memory Super-Cycle
long.p: Micron reported a record fiscal-Q3, with management citing more than 1,200 percent year-over-year earnings growth on surging AI-memory demand. The result confirms the multi-year memory up-cycle that underpins the broader semiconductor complex.

long.h: SK Hynix, One of 2026's Hottest Stocks, Plans a Roughly $30 Billion US Listing Next Month
long.p: Memory giant SK Hynix is reportedly preparing a Nasdaq listing as soon as next month, one of the largest in recent memory. The move underscores how strong AI-memory demand has become a capital-markets event, not just an earnings story.

long.h: Alphabet Set to Join the Dow on June 29, Replacing Verizon
long.p: Alphabet will enter the price-weighted Dow Jones Industrial Average before the June 29 open, becoming the fifth Magnificent Seven member in the index. Inclusion adds a durable passive-flow bid and cements GOOGL as a core large-cap holding.

long.h: Amazon Lifts India AI and Cloud Commitment to $48 Billion
long.p: Amazon will invest an additional $13 billion in India by 2030 to expand AI and cloud infrastructure, taking total planned spend to $48 billion. The commitment, alongside strong Prime Day demand, supports the long-term AWS and advertising thesis.

long.h: The Data-Center Boom Is Sparking a Third Wave of Inflation
long.p: Soaring demand for memory chips is pushing component prices higher, feeding a fresh inflation impulse just as the Fed turns more hawkish. The open question is whether AI's productivity gains arrive fast enough to temper it.

short.h: Gold, Silver and Bitcoin Tumble as the Debasement Trade Unwinds on a Hawkish Fed
short.p: Gold broke below $4,000 and silver fell under $60 (down about 6 percent) as markets repriced toward as many as three Fed rate hikes and the dollar hit a one-year high. Bitcoin slid in sympathy as the crowded debasement trade unwound.

short.h: Crude Slides as the Strait of Hormuz Reopens and China Weighs Resuming Iran Oil
short.p: Brent fell back toward pre-conflict levels after the Strait of Hormuz reopened and US-Iran tensions eased, draining the geopolitical risk premium that had lifted oil above $130. China's state refiners are now weighing a resumption of Iranian crude purchases.

short.h: Microsoft Slides 2 Percent in the AI Rotation; Bulls Flag a Rare Valuation Discount
short.p: Microsoft fell 2.3 percent as investors kept rotating out of AI software into beaten-down cyclicals. Bulls argue shares now trade 20 to 25 percent below five-year valuation averages even as performance obligations doubled to $633 billion.

short.h: Tesla Slides as Musk Briefly Loses Trillionaire Status; Q2 Deliveries Loom July 2
short.p: Tesla fell 1.6 percent with high-beta names as a drop in Tesla and SpaceX shares pushed Elon Musk back below trillionaire status. July 2 Q2 delivery numbers are the next catalyst for a volatile, optionality-driven holding.

short.h: After the Chip Sell-Off, Attention Turns Back to Quality as Micron Lifts Sentiment
short.p: With the Korea-led rout stabilizing, investors are re-rating quality semis on durable AI demand; Nvidia still posts ~75 percent gross margins and a stronger growth profile than peers. Micron's blowout brightens the near-term setup for a semis bounce.

notes.NVDA: Nvidia at $199.00, down 0.5 percent and holding the $200 line as the chip rout stabilized; Micron's blowout and a strong networking and data-center story keep the multi-year AI thesis intact after the pullback.
notes.MSFT: Microsoft at $365.46, down 2.3 percent as the rotation out of AI software extended; bulls flag shares 20 to 25 percent below five-year valuation averages despite performance obligations doubling to $633 billion.
notes.INTC: Intel at $131.65, down 0.5 percent and consolidating a huge year-to-date run after the rout; the historic Apple foundry deal remains the structural re-rating story.
notes.AVGO: Broadcom at $382.07, up 0.5 percent and steadying with the semis; multi-year custom-silicon and networking backlog underpin the AI case despite the recent guidance tantrum.
notes.QQQ: Nasdaq-100 ETF at $710.62, down 0.4 percent as megacap AI softness was partly offset by stabilizing chips and rotation into cyclicals.
notes.SMH: Semiconductor ETF at $618.92, down 0.5 percent and basing after the Korea-led rout; Micron's record quarter and SK Hynix's planned $30 billion US listing brighten the memory cycle.
notes.SPY: S&P 500 ETF at $733.24, essentially flat as defensive rotation and consumer strength offset AI weakness; broad breadth is cushioning the index.
notes.AAPL: Apple at $293.08, down 0.4 percent and relatively resilient; the Intel foundry tie-up and a near-record level keep the structural story intact.
notes.AMZN: Amazon at $234.27, up 0.1 percent and bucking the tape on strong Prime Day demand and a fresh $13 billion India AI and cloud commitment (now $48 billion); a cheap-versus-peers cloud thesis underpins the long-term case.
notes.META: Meta at $557.67, down 0.8 percent and holding up better than semis; the ad-and-AI engine is the support even as capex scrutiny lingers.
notes.GOOGL: Alphabet at $345.29, down 0.2 percent and a relative outperformer ahead of its June 29 entry into the Dow (replacing Verizon), which adds a structural passive-flow bid.
notes.TSLA: Tesla at $375.53, down 1.6 percent with high-beta names as Musk briefly lost trillionaire status; July 2 Q2 delivery numbers are the next catalyst for a volatile, optionality-driven holding.
notes.NFLX: Netflix at $71.84, down 1.4 percent and still about 30 percent below its highs since the Hastings board exit; bulls cite a historically low 20 to 22x multiple.
notes.DIS: Disney at $101.12, down 2.3 percent and giving back some defensive gains; parks and streaming profitability remain the swing factors under new CEO Josh D'Amaro.
notes.BRKB: Berkshire at $494.81, up 0.4 percent and doing its job as portfolio ballast, the quality bid that outperforms on risk-off days.
notes.DIA: Dow ETF at $518.52, up 0.4 percent and a relative haven; the index reshuffles June 29 as Alphabet replaces Verizon.
notes.OIL: USO at $106.29, down 4.5 percent as the Strait of Hormuz reopened and US-Iran tensions eased, draining the geopolitical risk premium; Brent is back near pre-conflict levels.
notes.BTC: Bitcoin near $60,983, down 2.7 percent and sliding into the low-60Ks as a hawkish Fed and a stronger dollar unwound the gold-silver-bitcoin debasement trade.
notes.GOLD: Gold near $4,009, down 3.4 percent and breaking below $4,000 for the first time since late 2025 as Fed rate-hike bets lifted the dollar to a one-year high.
notes.SILVER: Silver near $58.09, down 6.4 percent and the day's biggest loser, sliding below $60 as the debasement trade unwound; its industrial and solar demand link adds high-beta risk.
